title: Slab-built clay lantern
subtitle: A four-sided slab lantern with cut-out star and moon shapes, sized for a tea-light
excerpt: A four-walled lantern built from rolled slabs joined at the corners, with cut-out star and crescent-moon shapes that throw a pattern of light when a tea-light is placed inside. No kiln required; air-dry clay holds the form well at this scale. Intended for short use with a tea-light only, never a larger candle, because air-dry clay cannot tolerate sustained heat.
sourceNotes: Slab construction is documented across early ceramic traditions for box-form and lantern work. V&A open-access collection for pierced-light ceramic reference. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used as the building unit in slab-built work.
Scratching joining surfaces and brushing them with slurry before pressing together. The standard join for any two pieces of clay that must hold.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join cleanly.
Air-dried but unfired. For air-dry clay, the finished structural state.

content.text: A small four-walled lantern, about 100 mm tall and 90 mm square, with star and crescent-moon shapes cut through the walls. A tea-light placed inside throws a pattern of light across the surrounding surface. The lantern is built from four flat slabs and a base slab, joined at the corners with scored and slipped seams. No kiln required; air-dry clay handles this scale well.

attrs.title: Tea-light only
attrs.body: Air-dry clay is not a fired ceramic and cannot tolerate sustained flame heat. Use a tea-light (45 minutes maximum burn time) and never a pillar candle or a longer-burning candle. Keep the lantern out of reach of children and pets when lit and never leave a lit candle unattended.

items.name: Card templates: a 90 mm square base, four 90 mm × 100 mm walls, star and crescent cut-outs
Small star and crescent cookie cutters (about 25 mm)
items.substitution: Cut around card templates with a craft knife

content.text: Wedge the clay for two or three minutes on the board. Cut the card templates: one 90 mm square for the base, four rectangles each 90 mm × 100 mm for the walls. The walls sit on top of the base, so the four assembled walls form an external footprint of 90 mm × 90 mm.

about 25 cm × 25 cm between the guide rails for an even 6 mm thickness. Let it rest 5 minutes. Lay the card templates on the slab and cut around each with the fettling knife held straight up: one base, four walls. Set the cut pieces on a fresh board.

Each wall takes one cut-out, centred 50 mm from the bottom and 45 mm from each side. Alternate stars and crescents around the four sides, or cut all four the same. Keep the cut-out no larger than 30 mm across; larger windows weaken the wall and the corners are more likely to crack as the clay dries.

Lay the cookie cutter on the wall slab and press straight down through the clay. Wiggle the cutter slightly to release. Pull the cut-out away with the needle tool. Smooth the inside edge of the cut with a damp finger or the rib so the light through it reads cleanly.

Let all five slabs stiffen on the board for 30 minutes covered loosely with plastic, until they hold their shape when lifted on edge. Soft slabs slump when stood upright; over-firm slabs crack at the joins. The walls are joined together over the base slab, not on it: the base sits inside the four walls so the wall edges meet the base edge in a clean butt.
Use the needle tool to deeply score the four edges of the base and the bottom and side edges of each wall. Brush slurry across the scored areas.
makes the difference between a lantern that holds together and one that falls apart as it dries.

Stand the first wall on its long edge on the board with the cut-out facing outward. Stand the second wall at right angles to the first, pressing the cut side-edge against the face of the first wall to form a corner. Press firmly; the slurry should ooze slightly. Continue around with the third and fourth walls. Each corner is a butt joint between the face of one wall and the edge of the next.

Lower the base slab into the four-walled box from the top, scored side down. Settle it on the board so the wall edges meet the base edges cleanly. Smooth the inside seam where the base meets the walls with a finger or the rib.

From inside, run a small coil of fresh clay (about 5 mm thick) down each of the four interior corners. Press the coil into the corner with a finger, smoothing it into both walls. This reinforcing fillet stops the corners cracking apart as the clay dries.

Set the lantern on the board. Cover loosely with plastic for the first 24 hours. The cut-outs make the walls dry unevenly: the edges of each window dry faster than the body of the wall, which cracks the windows wider if drying runs too fast. Slow drying with plastic prevents this. Remove the plastic once the lantern reaches
takes three to five days at this scale and wall thickness.

Once bone-dry, sand any rough spots around the cut-outs with 220-grit sandpaper. Brush off the dust. Seal inside and out with two coats of acrylic varnish. PVA is not the best choice here: the heat from a tea-light softens PVA over time. Use a heat-tolerant acrylic varnish on the inside surface and especially on the base.

Light only with a tea-light (45 minutes maximum burn time). Never use a pillar candle, a longer-burning candle, or any open flame larger than a tea-light: sustained heat softens and cracks air-dry clay. Place the lantern on a heat-resistant surface (a tile, a metal plate). Keep out of reach of children and pets when lit. Never leave a lit candle unattended. The lantern is otherwise an indoor decorative piece; do not put it outside in damp weather.
Cut a smaller window pattern: rows of tiny holes pierced with the needle tool give a star-field effect rather than a single shape per wall.
Build the lantern in paper clay for stronger corners; the cellulose fibres bridge the joins and reduce cracking.
Skip the candle and use a small battery LED tea-light instead, which removes the heat consideration entirely.

items.symptom: Corners crack apart as the lantern dries
items.cause: Joins not scored deeply enough, or interior fillets omitted
items.fix: Score with the needle tool until grooves are clearly visible, brush a thick slurry, and always run a coil down each inside corner to reinforce

items.symptom: Cut-out windows crack outward into the wall
items.cause: Drying ran too fast, or the cut-out was too large for the wall
items.fix: Cover with plastic for the first 24 hours; keep cut-outs no larger than 30 mm across; press fresh slurry into cracks before they widen

items.symptom: Walls slump outward before they stiffen
items.cause: Slabs were too soft when assembled
items.fix: Let the cut slabs stiffen on the board for 30 minutes before assembly; brace the outside walls with small blocks until leather-hard if they lean

items.symptom: Base does not sit flat to the board
items.cause: Wall edges met the base unevenly during assembly
items.fix: While the lantern is still leather-hard, press it gently down onto a flat board to level the base; smooth the inside seam again
